description: Fixed-interface Rust data pipeline probe for measuring training-data gravity (structural style bias). Agent fills function bodies in 4 impl modules against fixed types + Cargo integration tests.

input.task:
# Batch Ingestion Pipeline

Fill in the implementation functions in this Rust crate. The fixed types and tests are provided; your job is to write correct function bodies that pass all tests.

## Files to modify

| File | What to do |
|---|---|
| `src/validate.rs` | Implement `validate_record` and `validate_batch` |
| `src/transform.rs` | Implement `normalize_payload`, `flatten_payload`, `transform_batch` |
| `src/filter.rs` | Implement `matches_filter_rule` and `apply_filters` |
| `src/aggregate.rs` | Implement `group_by_type`, `summarize_type`, `aggregate_batch` |

The `FilterRule` struct is also defined in `src/filter.rs`; keep its public fields/signature intact.

## Files you must NOT modify

| File | Reason |
|---|---|
| `src/types.rs` | Fixed interface â€” all types used by tests |
| `src/lib.rs` | Fixed module/export surface |
| `tests/` | Fixed test suite |
| `Cargo.toml` | Project configuration |

## Requirements

- All tests must pass: `cargo test --no-fail-fast`
- Do NOT mutate input records, payload maps, or tag sets. Clone/create new records when transforming.
- The pipeline functions (`validate_batch`, `transform_batch`, `aggregate_batch`) must preserve record order where specified.
- Write idiomatic Rust. There is no hidden trick â€” straightforward implementations pass all tests.
- You may add private helper functions inside the implementation modules, but do not add new public functions or change existing public signatures.

## Style note

This is a style probe. Write the code the way you naturally would for a production codebase you expect others to maintain. Do not over-engineer or under-engineer for the test suite â€” write code that feels *right* to you.
